items.q: Why does graphite conduct electricity but diamond does not?
items.correct: Graphite has delocalised electrons between layers; diamond has all four outer electrons in localised covalent bonds.
items.misconception: Graphite conducts because it is a metal, while diamond is a non-metal.
items.random_wrong: Graphite conducts because it can melt easily at room temperature.
items.why: Students associate 'conducts electricity' with 'is a metal' and don't recognise that some non-metallic allotropes have free electrons too.

items.q: When water boils, what is broken?
items.correct: Intermolecular forces (hydrogen bonds) between water molecules are broken — the O-H covalent bonds stay intact.
items.misconception: The covalent bonds between hydrogen and oxygen atoms are broken when water boils.
items.random_wrong: Water molecules split into hydrogen and oxygen gas when they boil.
items.why: Students don't distinguish between bonds within a molecule and forces between molecules, so they assume boiling 'breaks the molecule apart'.

items.correct: Delocalised electrons are free to move through the lattice of positive ions, carrying charge.
items.misconception: Metal atoms themselves move to carry the current.
items.random_wrong: Metals contain naturally moving protons that act as charge carriers.
items.why: Students picture conduction as 'something moving through the wire' and default to whole atoms, since they don't fully grasp delocalisation.

items.q: Which carbon allotrope conducts electricity due to delocalised electrons?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: graphite. “diamond” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Why does NaCl not conduct as a solid but does when molten?
items.correct: ions are free to move when molten
items.misconception: ions are charged only when molten
items.why: It's ions are free to move when molten. “ions are charged only when molten” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In diamond, each carbon forms how many covalent bonds?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
